content.subtitle: Use
items: Singular countable nouns (when not being specific or when mentioning something for the first time): There's **a** brilliant new café near the library.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Whether we use 'an' or 'a' depends on the sound, not the spelling.

examples.correct: Winning the prize was **a** unique honour.

examples.incorrect: Winning the prize was **an** unique honour.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Singular countable nouns (when being specific): Have you seen **the** documentary we discussed?
Singular countable nouns (when talking generally): **The** printing press changed the world when it was invented.
Plural countable nouns (when being specific): Nobody believed **the** stories about **the** local politician.
Uncountable nouns (when being specific): She took **the** advice her mentor had offered.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: The way we say 'the' changes depending on the sound at the start of the next word. Before a consonant sound (*the media*, *the USA*, *the programme*), we pronounce it /ðə/. Before a vowel sound (*the announcer*, *the actor*, *the editor*), we pronounce it /ði/.
We often use 'the' when we are talking about something there is only one of.
✓ **the** sky, **the** sun, **the** moon, **the** Prince of Wales, **the** North Pole, **the** World Cup

content.description: We often don't use an article at all. This is sometimes called the zero article.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Plural countable nouns (when talking generally): Reporters travel to conflict zones all over the world.
Uncountable nouns (when talking generally): Information spreads quickly through social media.

content.description: Study how articles are used in the following phrases and expressions. Each row shows a general pattern with examples. For instance, '**the** River Thames' illustrates that we always use 'the' before river names.

rows.label: Time
items: definite article: in the 1990s, in (the) summer, in the morning
zero article: in 2008, in June, on Friday, at night

rows.label: People and work
items: indefinite article: have a job, work as a ...
definite article: the queen, the principal, the president, the French
zero article: become president, go to work, be at work, have work to do, he's French

rows.label: Places
items: definite article: the Alps, the Atlantic Ocean, the River Thames, the Earth, the Arctic, the USA, the UK, the Cyclades
zero article: Mount Everest, Paris, America, Mars/Venus/Jupiter etc, Oxford Street, Lake Superior, Crete

rows.label: Entertainment and sport
items: definite article: the media, on the radio, play the guitar, go to the cinema, watch (the) TV
zero article: listen to music, on television, play tennis/football etc

rows.label: Organisations
items: definite article: the army, the police, the fire brigade

rows.label: Education
items: definite article: go to the school (as a visitor), be in the first year
zero article: go to school (as a student), be in Class 3B, maths

rows.label: Travel
items: indefinite article: take a taxi, catch a/the bus
definite article: on the bus
zero article: on foot, go home, go by bus

rows.label: Health
items: indefinite article: have a cold/headache/cough
definite article: have (the) flu/measles
zero article: have toothache

rows.label: Public buildings
items: definite article: the bank, the tax office, go to the hospital/prison (as a visitor)
zero article: go to hospital/prison/church (as a patient/prisoner/worshipper)

content.subtitle: American English note
content.description: US speakers use 'the hospital' where UK speakers say 'to hospital'.
examples: US: The doctor sent Maria to the hospital.  UK: The doctor sent Maria to hospital.

useItems: Use a singular or plural verb
examples: She wants to write for **a newspaper**.
There are **some** interesting **photos** in the gallery.
How **many programmes** do you watch each week?
How **much advice** did the doctor give you?

fullWidthRows: Common uncountable nouns: advice, coffee, furniture, glass, hair, homework, information, knowledge, luggage, money, news, paper, work

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Most uncountable nouns are singular, but a few are plural. These include *clothes, scissors, jeans, spectacles, trousers, groceries*, etc. With these words, we use a plural verb.
✓ Her new trousers **are** in the wash.
Some nouns are countable with one meaning and uncountable with another meaning.
✓ Could I have **a** clean **glass** for my water? (countable)
✓ The window is made of reinforced **glass**. (uncountable)

rows.use: countable nouns, usually in negative statements and questions
rows.example: There aren't **many** articles on this topic in the library.

rows.use: uncountable nouns, usually in negative statements and questions
rows.example: She doesn't spend **much** time watching television.

rows.use: countable and uncountable nouns in positive statements
rows.example:
This documentary has received **a lot of / lots of** praise.
What **a lot of** homework you've been given!
There have been **a few** changes to the broadcast schedule.
They say that **a little** patience goes a long way.
She has **few** opportunities to practise her photography.
The journalist had **little** evidence to support her story.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: The phrase '**only a few**' means 'not many'. The phrase '**only a little**' means 'not much'.
✓ There are **only a few** books left on the shelf.
✓ There's **only a little** time left, so let's hurry.

sections.title: A: Articles
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I always go jogging in **evening / the evening** before dinner.
Children in England often learn to play **cricket / the cricket** at primary school.
We missed **bus / the bus** home and had to walk.
Sarah left work early because she had **a headache / the headache** all morning.
The thief was sentenced to **prison / the prison** for eighteen months.
He spent the summer working as **waiter / a waiter** at a beach restaurant.
It was raining hard, so we decided to take **a taxi / the taxi** to the station.
She loves listening to **music / the music** while she cooks dinner.
Her weakest subject at university is **chemistry / the chemistry**.
The first commercial radio station launched in **1920s / in the 1920s**.

sections.title: B: Multiple choice (Articles)
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer. If no word is needed, choose 'D'.

items.sentence: Oh, I forgot to mention! She's bought ...... new phone.
Have you seen ...... keys I left here?
We've run out of ...... milk, so pick some up on your way home.
This award is ...... unique chance to showcase your talent.
Have a look — ...... screen on your laptop seems to be cracked.
It was ...... honour to be chosen to represent the school.
There's ...... real possibility that she'll win the competition.
The storm is slowly moving across ...... Atlantic.
She often works late at ...... night to meet her deadlines.
Scientists are studying whether life could ever exist on ...... Mars.

sections.title: C: Error correction (extra words - articles)
sections.instructions: Circle the extra word in each line. Click it.

items.sentence: [The] many young people use social media for hours each day.
They share [a] latest news, photos and videos with their friends.
Most experts agree it can be [the] difficult to focus on schoolwork
when your phone is so [a] close at hand.
Parents are concerned that [a] most teenagers prefer
to spend [the] time online rather than meeting their friends.
At [a] such times, having a digital detox is a wise move.
However, for [the] many families, enforcing screen-time limits
is a real challenge. Although [an] most parents try hard,
they know it is hard to fight against [the] peer pressure.

sections.title: D: Countable/uncountable (verb agreement)
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: The jeans I ordered online ...... (be) the wrong size.
Unfortunately, the advice the consultant gave us ...... (be) completely wrong.
Her new glasses ...... (be) exactly what she needed for reading.
The furniture in a large house ...... (take) forever to dust.
His hair ...... (look) completely different after the haircut.
She sensed that the information they had been given ...... (not / be) accurate.
His money ...... (be) in the safe, not the drawer.

sections.title: E: Error correction (countable/uncountable)
sections.instructions: Find ten mistakes in the passage and type the corrections in the gaps next to each bold error.
sections.passage: We interrupt your regular broadcast to report **an important news** (1) ...... We are receiving **informations** (2) ...... about an extraordinary event at the city museum. The display case, which was made entirely of **glasses** (3) ...... , has been broken into overnight. Security staff **was** (4) ...... shocked to discover that the ancient statue was missing. The statue had unusual features, including extremely long **hairs** (5) ...... and was found dressed in what appeared to be **a jean** (6) ...... **Much** (7) ...... witnesses have told police they saw nothing unusual. Investigators are hoping to share their **knowledges** (8) ...... with international experts. Police advice **are** (9) ...... to stay away from the area. We will return with **another news** (10) ...... as soon as we have it.

sections.title: F: Key Word Transformations (Quantifiers)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Not many people joined the community debate.
**only** There ...... people in the debate.

The survivor described the scene to the police reporter.
**a** The survivor ...... of the scene.

There isn't much flour left.
**a** There ...... flour left.

She is thinking of becoming a firefighter when she leaves school.
**brigade** She is thinking of ...... when she leaves school.

The announcement surprised us and we couldn't react.
**shocking** The announcement ...... couldn't react.

There aren't many good books published this month.
**only** There ...... good books published this month.

He didn't eat much breakfast before the exam.
**only** He ...... breakfast before the exam.

You have lots of books!
**lot** What ...... you have!
I have a lot on today, so I have **H** very little time to help you right now.
I'm pleased to say we have received **F** very few complaints from customers this month.
She got a promotion last month, so they have **G** a little money to spend on treats.
The beach looks cleaner now that **B** a few people have started picking up their litter.
They overspent last month, so they have **D** little money for anything extra.
The meeting finished early, so I have **A** a little time to catch up with you.
It's disappointing that **E** few people attended the community clean-up event.
Sadly, the restaurant has received **C** a few complaints about the slow service.
If you have ...... spare time before leaving, double-check the report.
Wait ...... moments — I just need to find my keys.
There's only ...... butter left, so pick some up at the market.
With ...... effort each day, you will quickly improve your writing.
It was a shame that ...... of the audience stayed until the end.
Add ...... salt to the pasta water before cooking.
At this point, there is ...... chance of changing the outcome.
Can you explain ...... points from the lecture I missed?
There's ...... juice in the fridge, if anyone is thirsty.
The course is so difficult that ...... students pass on the first attempt.
I'll be there soon — I just have ...... things to finish at the office.
Some students feel there's ...... point in revising when they already know the topic.

sections.title: I: Cloze (Articles)
sections.instructions: Write an article in each gap where necessary. If an article is not necessary, write a dash (-).

items.sentence: Ben: Hello? Anna: Hi, is that Ben? It's (1) ...... Anna.
Anna: Yes! Hi. How was (2) ...... concert?
Ben: Amazing! We took (3) ...... taxi to (4) ...... stadium, but (5) ...... driver got completely lost...
...so we nearly missed (6) ...... opening act.
Anna: Oh no! What did you do (7) ...... last night, then?
Ben: Well, I just stayed at (8) ...... home. I listened to (9) ...... music and then caught (10) ...... news.
Anna: Really? So what's happening in (11) ...... world?
Ben: Well, I know you don't follow (12) ...... politics, but there was (13) ...... update on (14) ...... climate change.
It gave me (15) ...... few ideas for (16) ...... science project we were discussing (17) ...... other day.
Ben: Oh, by the way — (18) ...... lead singer of your favourite band has just released (19) ...... album. It's (20) ...... one you've been waiting for!
Anna: What?! I need to check (21) ...... Internet right now! See you at (22) ...... school tomorrow. Bye!

sections.title: J: Error correction (Articles)
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences correctly, adding articles where necessary.

items.context: We went to lecture hall and heard great talk, though some of audience was bit restless.
items.answer: We went to the lecture hall and heard a great talk, though some of the audience was a bit restless.

items.context: Give me ring tomorrow and I'll meet you at station.
items.answer: Give me a ring tomorrow and I'll meet you at the station.

items.context: Editor announced at meeting that team wanted to launch new website.
items.answer: The editor announced at the meeting that the team wanted to launch a new website.

items.context: He asked hotel receptionist where pool was and was told it was on third floor.
items.answer: He asked the hotel receptionist where the pool was and was told it was on the third floor.

items.context: It was reported on news on radio that Lake Baikal, deepest freshwater lake on Earth, is threatened by pollution.
items.answer: It was reported on the news on the radio that Lake Baikal, the deepest freshwater lake on Earth, is threatened by pollution.

items.context: Internet has revolutionised communication and is now vital to economy of every country.
items.answer: The Internet has revolutionised communication and is now vital to the economy of every country.

items.context: Presenter welcomed guest warmly and then began interview straight away.
items.answer: The presenter welcomed the guest warmly and then began the interview straight away.

items.context: Career in media can be quite challenging because of pressure you are under to meet deadlines.
items.answer: A career in the media can be quite challenging because of the pressure you are under to meet deadlines.

items.context: Very few people in 19th century had access to reliable news, and most lived quite simple life.
items.answer: Very few people in the 19th century had access to reliable news, and most lived quite a simple life.

items.context: I have more free time now, so I am thinking of trying new activity, like learning musical instrument.
items.answer: I have more free time now, so I am thinking of trying a new activity, like learning a musical instrument.
